{{Infobox ancient site | name = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | image = [[File:Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki), funkyu.jpg|280px]] | caption = Tumulus and entrance to the stone chamber | alias = | part_of = {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}} | location = {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Kokubu Motomura Fure 1206-1 (Iwayamori District), Nagasaki Prefecture | latitude_deg = 33 | latitude_min = 48 | latitude_sec = 4.57 | longitude_deg = 129 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 45.42 | ISO = JP-42 | form = {{ill|Circular burial mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} | dimensions = Diameter 45 m
Height 13.5 m | burial_facility = Dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
(possibly with a component stone {{ill|box-shaped sarcophagus|ja|箱式石棺}}) | artifacts = Iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, Silla pottery | associated_mounds = | construction_date = Late 6th century | interred = | designation = Included in the National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}} "Iki Kofun Group" | cultural_property = | notes = | map = Japan Nagasaki | icon = Burial mound | label = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | label_position = bottom }} '''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) is a {{ill|circular burial mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Nagasaki Prefecture. It is one of the tumuli comprising the {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}}, designated as a National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}. == Overview == The tumulus is situated atop a gently sloping ridge in central Iki Island at an elevation of approximately 100 meters. By the late Edo period, the stone chamber had already been exposed, with detailed surveys conducted in {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}, {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}, and {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}. Excavations in {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}} also revealed notable features. The mound has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of approximately 13.5 meters. The burial facility is a dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, oriented southward. It consists of three compartments: a main chamber, a middle chamber (first antechamber), and a front chamber (second antechamber), connected to a corridor. This grand stone chamber measures a total length of 16.5 meters and features massive basalt stones as construction material. Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12年}}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62年}}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Museum|ja|壱岐市立一支国博物館}} (Iki City, Ashibe Town) == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Ashibe Town Board of Education, ''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun Environmental Improvement Project Report,'' 1990. * Iki {{Infobox ancient site | name = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | image = [[File:Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki), funkyu.jpg|280px]] | caption = Tumulus and entrance to the stone chamber | alias = | part_of = {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}} | location = {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Kokubu Motomura Fure 1206-1 (Iwayamori District), Nagasaki Prefecture | latitude_deg = 33 | latitude_min = 48 | latitude_sec = 4.57 | longitude_deg = 129 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 45.42 | ISO = JP-42 | form = {{ill|Circular burial mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} | dimensions = Diameter 45 m
Height 13.5 m | burial_facility = Dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
(possibly with a component stone {{ill|box-shaped sarcophagus|ja|箱式石棺}}) | artifacts = Iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, Silla pottery | associated_mounds = | construction_date = Late 6th century | interred = | designation = Included in the National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}} "Iki Kofun Group" | cultural_property = | notes = | map = Japan Nagasaki | icon = Burial mound | label = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | label_position = bottom }} '''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) is a {{ill|circular burial mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Nagasaki Prefecture. It is one of the tumuli comprising the {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}}, designated as a National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}. == Overview == The tumulus is situated atop a gently sloping ridge in central Iki Island at an elevation of approximately 100 meters. By the late Edo period, the stone chamber had already been exposed, with detailed surveys conducted in {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}, {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}, and {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}. Excavations in {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}} also revealed notable features. The mound has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of approximately 13.5 meters. The burial facility is a dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, oriented southward. It consists of three compartments: a main chamber, a middle chamber (first antechamber), and a front chamber (second antechamber), connected to a corridor. This grand stone chamber measures a total length of 16.5 meters and features massive basalt stones as construction material. Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12年}}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62年}}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Museum|ja|壱岐市立一支国博物館}} (Iki City, Ashibe Town) == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Ashibe Town Board of Education, ''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun Environmental Improvement Project Report,'' 1990. * Iki City Board of Education, ''Tumulus Groups on Iki Island: Current Survey,'' 2012. * Kyushu University, ''Archaeological Report of Ikihara Sites,'' 2018. * Togo Yoshida, ''The Great Japanese Gazetteer, Volume 7: Ōu,'' 1902. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki)}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/645 Iki Kofun Group - Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage It is one of the tumuli comprising the {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}}, designated as a National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}. == Overview == The tumulus is situated atop a gently sloping ridge in central Iki Island at an elevation of approximately 100 meters. By the late Edo period, the stone chamber had already been exposed, with detailed surveys conducted in {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}, {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}, and {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}. Excavations in {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}} also revealed notable features. The mound has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of approximately 13.5 meters. The burial facility is a dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, oriented southward. It consists of three compartments: a main chamber, a middle chamber (first antechamber), and a front chamber (second antechamber), connected to a corridor. This grand stone chamber measures a total length of 16.5 meters and features massive basalt stones as construction material. Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12年}}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62年}}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Museum|ja|壱岐市立一支国博物館}} (Iki City, Ashibe Town) == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Ashibe Town Board of Education, ''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun Environmental Improvement Project Report,'' 1990. * Iki City Board of Education, ''Tumulus Groups on Iki Island: Current Survey,'' 2012. * Kyushu University, ''Archaeological Report of Ikihara Sites,'' 2018. * Togo Yoshida, ''The Great Japanese Gazetteer, {{Infobox ancient site | name = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | image = [[File:Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki), funkyu.jpg|280px]] | caption = Tumulus and entrance to the stone chamber | alias = | part_of = {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}} | location = {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Kokubu Motomura Fure 1206-1 (Iwayamori District), Nagasaki Prefecture | latitude_deg = 33 | latitude_min = 48 | latitude_sec = 4.57 | longitude_deg = 129 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 45.42 | ISO = JP-42 | form = {{ill|Circular burial mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} | dimensions = Diameter 45 m
Height 13.5 m | burial_facility = Dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
(possibly with a component stone {{ill|box-shaped sarcophagus|ja|箱式石棺}}) | artifacts = Iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, Silla pottery | associated_mounds = | construction_date = Late 6th century | interred = | designation = Included in the National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}} "Iki Kofun Group" | cultural_property = | notes = | map = Japan Nagasaki | icon = Burial mound | label = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | label_position = bottom }} '''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) is a {{ill|circular burial mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Nagasaki Prefecture. It is one of the tumuli comprising the {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}}, designated as a National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}. == Overview == The tumulus is situated atop a gently sloping ridge in central Iki Island at an elevation of approximately 100 meters. By the late Edo period, the stone chamber had already been exposed, with detailed surveys conducted in {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}, {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}, and {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}. Excavations in {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}} also revealed notable features. The mound has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of approximately 13.5 meters. The burial facility is a dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, oriented southward. It consists of three compartments: a main chamber, a middle chamber (first antechamber), and a front chamber (second antechamber), connected to a corridor. This grand stone chamber measures a total length of 16.5 meters and features massive basalt stones as construction material. Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12年}}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62年}}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Museum|ja|壱岐市立一支国博物館}} (Iki City, Ashibe Town) == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Ashibe Town Board of Education, ''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun Environmental Improvement Project Report,'' 1990. * Iki City Board of Education, ''Tumulus Groups on Iki Island: Current Survey,'' 2012. * Kyushu University, ''Archaeological Report of Ikihara Sites,'' 2018. * Togo Yoshida, ''The Great Japanese Gazetteer, Volume 7: Ōu,'' 1902. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki)}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/645 Iki Kofun Group - Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage Database] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Iki Kofun Group]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Round Kofun]] In {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12年}}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62年}}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Museum|ja|壱岐市立一支国博物館}} (Iki City, Ashibe Town) == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Ashibe Town Board of Education, ''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun Environmental Improvement Project Report,'' 1990. * Iki {{Infobox ancient site | name = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | image = [[File:Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki), funkyu.jpg|280px]] | caption = Tumulus and entrance to the stone chamber | alias = | part_of = {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}} | location = {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Kokubu Motomura Fure 1206-1 (Iwayamori District), Nagasaki Prefecture | latitude_deg = 33 | latitude_min = 48 | latitude_sec = 4.57 | longitude_deg = 129 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 45.42 | ISO = JP-42 | form = {{ill|Circular burial mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} | dimensions = Diameter 45 m
Height 13.5 m | burial_facility = Dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
(possibly with a component stone {{ill|box-shaped sarcophagus|ja|箱式石棺}}) | artifacts = Iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, Silla pottery | associated_mounds = | construction_date = Late 6th century | interred = | designation = Included in the National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}} "Iki Kofun Group" | cultural_property = | notes = | map = Japan Nagasaki | icon = Burial mound | label = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | label_position = bottom }} '''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) is a {{ill|circular burial mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Nagasaki Prefecture. It is one of the tumuli comprising the {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}}, designated as a National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}. == Overview == The tumulus is situated atop a gently sloping ridge in central Iki Island at an elevation of approximately 100 meters. By the late Edo period, the stone chamber had already been exposed, with detailed surveys conducted in {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}, {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}, and {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}. Excavations in {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}} also revealed notable features. The mound has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of approximately 13.5 meters. The burial facility is a dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, oriented southward. It consists of three compartments: a main chamber, a middle chamber (first antechamber), and a front chamber (second antechamber), connected to a corridor. This grand stone chamber measures a total length of 16.5 meters and features massive basalt stones as construction material. Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12年}}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62年}}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku {{Infobox ancient site | name = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | image = [[File:Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki), funkyu.jpg|280px]] | caption = Tumulus and entrance to the stone chamber | alias = | part_of = {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}} | location = {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Kokubu Motomura Fure 1206-1 (Iwayamori District), Nagasaki Prefecture | latitude_deg = 33 | latitude_min = 48 | latitude_sec = 4.57 | longitude_deg = 129 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 45.42 | ISO = JP-42 | form = {{ill|Circular burial mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} | dimensions = Diameter 45 m
Height 13.5 m | burial_facility = Dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
(possibly with a component stone {{ill|box-shaped sarcophagus|ja|箱式石棺}}) | artifacts = Iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, Silla pottery | associated_mounds = | construction_date = Late 6th century | interred = | designation = Included in the National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}} "Iki Kofun Group" | cultural_property = | notes = | map = Japan Nagasaki | icon = Kofun | label = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | label_position = bottom }} '''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) is a {{ill|circular burial mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Nagasaki Prefecture. It is one of the tumuli comprising the {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}}, designated as a National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}. == Overview == The tumulus is situated atop a gently sloping ridge in central Iki Island at an elevation of approximately 100 meters. By the late Edo period, the stone chamber had already been exposed, with detailed surveys conducted in {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}, {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}, and {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}. Excavations in {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}} also revealed notable features. The mound has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of approximately 13.5 meters. The burial facility is a dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, oriented southward. It consists of three compartments: a main chamber, a middle chamber (first antechamber), and a front chamber (second antechamber), connected to a corridor. This grand stone chamber measures a total length of 16.5 meters and features massive basalt stones as construction material. Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12年}}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62年}}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Museum|ja|壱岐市立一支国博物館}} (Iki City, Ashibe Town) == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Ashibe Town Board of Education, ''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun Environmental Improvement Project Report,'' 1990. * Iki City Board of Education, ''Tumulus Groups on Iki Island: Current Survey,'' 2012. * Kyushu University, ''Archaeological Report of Ikihara Sites,'' 2018. * Togo Yoshida, ''The Great Japanese Gazetteer, Volume 7: Ōu,'' 1902. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki)}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/645 Iki Kofun Group - Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage {{Infobox ancient site | name = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | image = [[File:Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki), funkyu.jpg|280px]] | caption = Kofun and entrance to the stone chamber | alias = | part_of = {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}} | location = {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Kokubu Motomura Fure 1206-1 (Iwayamori District), Nagasaki Prefecture | latitude_deg = 33 | latitude_min = 48 | latitude_sec = 4.57 | longitude_deg = 129 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 45.42 | ISO = JP-42 | form = {{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} | dimensions = Diameter 45 m
Height 13.5 m | burial_facility = Dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
(possibly with a component stone {{ill|box-shaped sarcophagus|ja|箱式石棺}}) | artifacts = Iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, Silla pottery | associated_mounds = | construction_date = Late 6th century | interred = | designation = Included in the National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}} "Iki Kofun Group" | cultural_property = | notes = | map = Japan Nagasaki | icon = Kofun | label = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | label_position = bottom }} '''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) is a {{ill|circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Nagasaki Prefecture. It is one of the tumuli comprising the {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}}, designated as a National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}. == Overview == The tumulus is situated atop a gently sloping ridge in central Iki Island at an elevation of approximately 100 meters. By the late Edo period, the stone chamber had already been exposed, with detailed surveys conducted in {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}, {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}, and {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}. Excavations in {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}} also revealed notable features. The mound has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of approximately 13.5 meters. The burial facility is a dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, oriented southward. It consists of three compartments: a main chamber, a middle chamber (first antechamber), and a front chamber (second antechamber), connected to a corridor. This grand stone chamber measures a total length of 16.5 meters and features massive basalt stones as construction material. Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12年}}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62年}}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Museum|ja|壱岐市立一支国博物館}} (Iki City, Ashibe Town) == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Ashibe Town Board of Education, ''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun Environmental Improvement Project Report,'' 1990. * Iki City Board of Education, ''Kofun Groups on Iki Island: Current Survey,'' 2012. * Kyushu University, ''Archaeological Report of Ikihara Sites,'' 2018. * Togo Yoshida, ''The Great Japanese Gazetteer, Volume 7: Ōu,'' 1902. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki)}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/645 Iki Kofun Group - Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage In {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12年}}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62年}}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Museum|ja|壱岐市立一支国博物館}} (Iki City, Ashibe Town) == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Ashibe Town Board of Education, ''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun Environmental Improvement Project Report,'' 1990. * Iki {{Infobox ancient site | name = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | image = [[File:Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki), funkyu.jpg|280px]] | caption = Kofun and entrance to the stone chamber | alias = | part_of = {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}} | location = {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Kokubu Motomura Fure 1206-1 (Iwayamori District), Nagasaki Prefecture | latitude_deg = 33 | latitude_min = 48 | latitude_sec = 4.57 | longitude_deg = 129 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 45.42 | ISO = JP-42 | form = {{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} | dimensions = Diameter 45 m
Height 13.5 m | burial_facility = Dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
(possibly with a component stone {{ill|box-shaped sarcophagus|ja|箱式石棺}}) | artifacts = Iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, Silla pottery | associated_mounds = | construction_date = Late 6th century | interred = | designation = Included in the National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}} "Iki Kofun Group" | cultural_property = | notes = | map = Japan Nagasaki | icon = Kofun | label = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | label_position = bottom }} '''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) is a {{ill|circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Nagasaki Prefecture. It is one of the tumuli comprising the {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}}, designated as a National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}. == Overview == The tumulus is situated atop a gently sloping ridge in central Iki Island at an elevation of approximately 100 meters. By the late Edo period, the stone chamber had already been exposed, with detailed surveys conducted in {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}, {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}, and {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}. Excavations in {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}} also revealed notable features. The mound has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of approximately 13.5 meters. The burial facility is a dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, oriented southward. It consists of three compartments: a main chamber, a middle chamber (first antechamber), and a front chamber (second antechamber), connected to a corridor. This grand stone chamber measures a total length of 16.5 meters and features massive basalt stones as construction material. Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12年}}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62年}}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} | name = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | image = [[File:Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki), funkyu.jpg|280px]] | caption = Kofun and entrance to the stone chamber | alias = | part_of = {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}} | location = {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Kokubu Motomura Fure 1206-1 (Iwayamori District), Nagasaki Prefecture | latitude_deg = 33 | latitude_min = 48 | latitude_sec = 4.57 | longitude_deg = 129 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 45.42 | ISO = JP-42 | form = {{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} | dimensions = Diameter 45 m
Height 13.5 m | burial_facility = Dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
(possibly with a component stone {{ill|box-shaped sarcophagus|ja|箱式石棺}}) | artifacts = Iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, Silla pottery | associated_mounds = | construction_date = Late 6th century | interred = | designation = Included in the National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}} "Iki Kofun Group" | cultural_property = | notes = | map = Japan Nagasaki | icon = Kofun | label = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | label_position = bottom }} '''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) is a {{ill|circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Nagasaki Prefecture. It is one of the tumuli comprising the {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}}, designated as a National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}. == Overview == The tumulus is situated atop a gently sloping ridge in central Iki Island at an elevation of approximately 100 meters. By the late Edo period, the stone chamber had already been exposed, with detailed surveys conducted in {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}, {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}, and {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}. Excavations in {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}} also revealed notable features. The mound has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of approximately 13.5 meters. The burial facility is a dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, oriented southward. It consists of three compartments: a main chamber, a middle chamber (first antechamber), and a front chamber (second antechamber), connected to a corridor. This grand stone chamber measures a total length of 16.5 meters and features massive basalt stones as construction material. Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12年}}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62年}}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Museum|ja|壱岐市立一支国博物館}} (Iki City, Ashibe Town) == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Ashibe Town Board of Education, ''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun Environmental Improvement Project Report,'' 1990. * Iki City Board of Education, ''Kofun Groups on Iki Island: Current Survey,'' 2012. * Kyushu University, ''Archaeological Report of Ikihara Sites,'' 2018. * Togo Yoshida, ''The Great Japanese Gazetteer, Volume 7: Ōu,'' 1902. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki)}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/645 Iki Kofun Group - Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} | name = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | image = [[File:Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki), funkyu.jpg|280px]] | caption = Kofun and entrance to the stone chamber | alias = | part_of = {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}} | location = {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Kokubu Motomura Fure 1206-1 (Iwayamori District), Nagasaki Prefecture | latitude_deg = 33 | latitude_min = 48 | latitude_sec = 4.57 | longitude_deg = 129 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 45.42 | ISO = JP-42 | form = {{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} | dimensions = Diameter 45 m
Height 13.5 m | burial_facility = Dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
(possibly with a component stone {{ill|box-shaped sarcophagus|ja|箱式石棺}}) | artifacts = Iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}}, Silla pottery | associated_mounds = | construction_date = Late 6th century | interred = | designation = Included in the National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}} "Iki Kofun Group" | cultural_property = | notes = | map = Japan Nagasaki | icon = Kofun | label = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | label_position = bottom }} '''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) is a {{ill|circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Nagasaki Prefecture. It is one of the tumuli comprising the {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}}, designated as a National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}. == Overview == The tumulus is situated atop a gently sloping ridge in central Iki Island at an elevation of approximately 100 meters. By the late Edo period, the stone chamber had already been exposed, with detailed surveys conducted in {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}, {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}, and {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}. Excavations in {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}} also revealed notable features. The mound has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of approximately 13.5 meters. The burial facility is a dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, oriented southward. It consists of three compartments: a main chamber, a middle chamber (first antechamber), and a front chamber (second antechamber), connected to a corridor. This grand stone chamber measures a total length of 16.5 meters and features massive basalt stones as construction material. Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12年}}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62年}}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Museum|ja|壱岐市立一支国博物館}} (Iki City, Ashibe Town) == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Ashibe Town Board of Education, ''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun Environmental Improvement Project Report,'' 1990. * Iki City Board of Education, ''Kofun Groups on Iki Island: Current Survey,'' 2012. * Kyushu University, ''Archaeological Report of Ikihara Sites,'' 2018. * Togo Yoshida, ''The Great Japanese Gazetteer, Volume 7: Ōu,'' 1902. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki)}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/645 Iki Kofun Group - Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage Database] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Iki Kofun Group]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Round Kofun]] {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} | name = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | image = [[File:Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki), funkyu.jpg|280px]] | caption = Kofun and entrance to the stone chamber | alias = | part_of = {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}} | location = {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Kokubu Motomura Fure 1206-1 (Iwayamori District), Nagasaki Prefecture | latitude_deg = 33 | latitude_min = 48 | latitude_sec = 4.57 | longitude_deg = 129 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 45.42 | ISO = JP-42 | form = {{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} | dimensions = Diameter 45 m

Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12年}}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62年}}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Museum|ja|壱岐市立一支国博物館}} (Iki City, Ashibe Town) == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Ashibe Town Board of Education, ''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun Environmental Improvement Project Report,'' 1990. * Iki City Board of Education, ''Kofun Groups on Iki Island: Current Survey,'' 2012. * Kyushu University, ''Archaeological Report of Ikihara Sites,'' 2018. * Togo Yoshida, ''The Great Japanese Gazetteer, Volume 7: Ōu,'' 1902. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki)}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/645 Iki Kofun Group - Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage (possibly with a component stone {{ill|box-shaped sarcophagus|ja|箱式石棺}}) | artifacts = Iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}}, Silla pottery | associated_mounds = | construction_date = Late 6th century | interred = | designation = Included in the National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}} "Iki Kofun Group" | cultural_property = | notes = | map = Japan Nagasaki | icon = Kofun | label = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | label_position = bottom }} '''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) is a {{ill|circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Nagasaki Prefecture. It is one of the tumuli comprising the {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}}, designated as a National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}. == Overview == The tumulus is situated atop a gently sloping ridge in central Iki Island at an elevation of approximately 100 meters. By the late Edo period, the stone chamber had already been exposed, with detailed surveys conducted in {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}, {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}, and {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}. Excavations in {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}} also revealed notable features. The mound has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of approximately 13.5 meters. The burial facility is a dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, oriented southward. It consists of three compartments: a main chamber, a middle chamber (first antechamber), and a front chamber (second antechamber), connected to a corridor. This grand stone chamber measures a total length of 16.5 meters and features massive basalt stones as construction material. Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12年}}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62年}}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} | name = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | image = [[File:Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki), funkyu.jpg|280px]] | caption = Kofun and entrance to the stone chamber | alias = | part_of = {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}} | location = {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Kokubu Motomura Fure 1206-1 (Iwayamori District), Nagasaki Prefecture | latitude_deg = 33 | latitude_min = 48 | latitude_sec = 4.57 | longitude_deg = 129 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 45.42 | ISO = JP-42 | form = {{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} | dimensions = Diameter 45 m
Height 13.5 m | burial_facility = Dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
(possibly with a component stone {{ill|box-shaped sarcophagus|ja|箱式石棺}}) | artifacts = Iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}}, Silla pottery | associated_mounds = | construction_date = Late 6th century | interred = | designation = Included in the National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}} "Iki Kofun Group" | cultural_property = | notes = | map = Japan Nagasaki | icon = Kofun | label = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | label_position = bottom }} '''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) is a {{ill|circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Nagasaki Prefecture. It is one of the tumuli comprising the {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}}, designated as a National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}. == Overview == The tumulus is situated atop a gently sloping ridge in central Iki Island at an elevation of approximately 100 meters. By the late Edo period, the stone chamber had already been exposed, with detailed surveys conducted in {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}, {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}, and {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}. Excavations in {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}} also revealed notable features. The mound has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of approximately 13.5 meters. The burial facility is a dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, oriented southward. It consists of three compartments: a main chamber, a middle chamber (first antechamber), and a front chamber (second antechamber), connected to a corridor. This grand stone chamber measures a total length of 16.5 meters and features massive basalt stones as construction material. Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12年}}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11年}}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62年}}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21年}}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Museum|ja|壱岐市立一支国博物館}} (Iki City, Ashibe Town) == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Ashibe Town Board of Education, ''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun Environmental Improvement Project Report,'' 1990. * Iki City Board of Education, ''Kofun Groups on Iki Island: Current Survey,'' 2012. * Kyushu University, ''Archaeological Report of Ikihara Sites,'' 2018. * Togo Yoshida, ''The Great Japanese Gazetteer, Volume 7: Ōu,'' 1902. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki)}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/645 Iki Kofun Group - Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} | name = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | image = [[File:Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki), funkyu.jpg|280px]] | caption = Kofun and entrance to the stone chamber | alias = | part_of = {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}} | location = {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Kokubu Motomura Fure 1206-1 (Iwayamori District), Nagasaki Prefecture | latitude_deg = 33 | latitude_min = 48 | latitude_sec = 4.57 | longitude_deg = 129 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 45.42 | ISO = JP-42 | form = {{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} | dimensions = Diameter 45 m
Height 13.5 m | burial_facility = Dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
(possibly with a component stone {{ill|box-shaped sarcophagus|ja|箱式石棺}}) | artifacts = Iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}}, Silla pottery | associated_mounds = | construction_date = Late 6th century | interred = | designation = Included in the National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}} "Iki Kofun Group" | cultural_property = | notes = | map = Japan Nagasaki | icon = Kofun | label = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | label_position = bottom }} '''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) is a {{ill|circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Nagasaki Prefecture. It is one of the tumuli comprising the {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}}, designated as a National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}. == Overview == The tumulus is situated atop a gently sloping ridge in central Iki Island at an elevation of approximately 100 meters. By the late Edo period, the stone chamber had already been exposed, with detailed surveys conducted in {{nihongo|1936|昭和11th year }}, {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}, and {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}. Excavations in {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}} also revealed notable features. The mound has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of approximately 13.5 meters. The burial facility is a dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, oriented southward. It consists of three compartments: a main chamber, a middle chamber (first antechamber), and a front chamber (second antechamber), connected to a corridor. This grand stone chamber measures a total length of 16.5 meters and features massive basalt stones as construction material. Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21st year }}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12nd year }}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11th year }}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28年}}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45年}}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55年}}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62nd year }}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21st year }}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Museum|ja|壱岐市立一支国博物館}} (Iki City, Ashibe Town) == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Ashibe Town Board of Education, ''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun Environmental Improvement Project Report,'' 1990. * Iki City Board of Education, ''Kofun Groups on Iki Island: Current Survey,'' 2012. * Kyushu University, ''Archaeological Report of Ikihara Sites,'' 2018. * Togo Yoshida, ''The Great Japanese Gazetteer, Volume 7: Ōu,'' 1902. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki)}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/645 Iki Kofun Group - Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage By the late Edo period, the stone chamber had already been exposed, with detailed surveys conducted in {{nihongo|1936|昭和11th year }}, {{nihongo|1970|昭和45th year }}, and {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}. Excavations in {{nihongo|1953|昭和28th year }} also revealed notable features. The mound has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of approximately 13.5 meters. The burial facility is a dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, oriented southward. It consists of three compartments: a main chamber, a middle chamber (first antechamber), and a front chamber (second antechamber), connected to a corridor. This grand stone chamber measures a total length of 16.5 meters and features massive basalt stones as construction material. Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21st year }}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12nd year }}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11th year }}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28th year }}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30年}}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36年}}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45th year }}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55th year }}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62nd year }}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21st year }}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Museum|ja|壱岐市立一支国博物館}} (Iki City, Ashibe Town) == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Ashibe Town Board of Education, ''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun Environmental Improvement Project Report,'' 1990. * Iki City Board of Education, ''Kofun Groups on Iki Island: Current Survey,'' 2012. * Kyushu University, ''Archaeological Report of Ikihara Sites,'' 2018. * Togo Yoshida, ''The Great Japanese Gazetteer, Volume 7: Ōu,'' 1902. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki)}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/645 Iki Kofun Group - Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage revision.comment: Text replacement - "there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure" to "there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure"
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} | name = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | image = [[File:Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki), funkyu.jpg|280px]] | caption = Kofun and entrance to the stone chamber | alias = | part_of = {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}} | location = {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Kokubu Motomura Fure 1206-1 (Iwayamori District), Nagasaki Prefecture | latitude_deg = 33 | latitude_min = 48 | latitude_sec = 4.57 | longitude_deg = 129 | longitude_min = 42 | longitude_sec = 45.42 | ISO = JP-42 | form = {{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} | dimensions = Diameter 45 m
Height 13.5 m | burial_facility = Dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
(possibly with a component stone {{ill|box-shaped sarcophagus|ja|箱式石棺}}) | artifacts = Iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}}, Silla pottery | associated_mounds = | construction_date = Late 6th century | interred = | designation = Included in the National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}} "Iki Kofun Group" | cultural_property = | notes = | map = Japan Nagasaki | icon = Kofun | label = Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun | label_position = bottom }} '''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) is a {{ill|circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|ja|壱岐市}}, Ashibe Town, Nagasaki Prefecture. It is one of the tumuli comprising the {{ill|Iki Kofun Group|ja|壱岐古墳群}}, designated as a National {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}. == Overview == The tumulus is situated atop a gently sloping ridge in central Iki Island at an elevation of approximately 100 meters. By the late Edo period, the stone chamber had already been exposed, with detailed surveys conducted in {{nihongo|1936|昭和11th year }}, {{nihongo|1970|昭和45th year }}, and {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}. Excavations in {{nihongo|1953|昭和28th year }} also revealed notable features. The mound has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of approximately 13.5 meters. The burial facility is a dual-sleeve {{ill|horizontal stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, oriented southward. It consists of three compartments: a main chamber, a middle chamber (first antechamber), and a front chamber (second antechamber), connected to a corridor. This grand stone chamber measures a total length of 16.5 meters and features massive basalt stones as construction material. Excavations have uncovered iron arrowheads, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}}, and Silla pottery. The mound is dated to the late 6th century, with some burials possibly extending into the 7th century. In {{nihongo|2009|平成21st year }}, the site was designated as part of the National Historic Site "Iki Kofun Group." == Archaeological History == * {{nihongo|1841|天保12nd year }}: The ''Kōshi Yawa'' includes illustrations of the tumulus and its stone chamber. * {{nihongo|1861|文久元年}}: The ''Iki Meishō Zushi'' contains sketches and measurements of the mound. * {{nihongo|1936|昭和11th year }}: Survey of the stone chamber conducted by Kyoto University (unpublished). * {{nihongo|1953|昭和28th year }}: Excavations by the Tōa Archaeological Society, with findings reported in {{nihongo|2018|平成30th year }}. * {{nihongo|1961|昭和36th year }}: Designated as a Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site. * {{nihongo|1970|昭和45th year }}: Survey of the stone chamber by Kyushu University, with detailed diagrams published in {{nihongo|1980|昭和55th year }}. * {{nihongo|1987|昭和62nd year }}: Typhoon damage to the right wall of the chamber entrance. * {{nihongo|1989|平成元年}}: Restoration and further investigations by Ashibe Town Board of Education. * {{nihongo|2009|平成21st year }}: Included in the National Historic Site designation. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a three-chamber structure, with details as follows: * Total chamber length: 16.5 m * Main chamber: 3.2 m long, 3 m wide, 3.3 m high * Middle chamber: 4.1 m long, 2.4 m wide, 1.75 m high * Front chamber: 4.1 m long, 2 m wide, 1.9 m high * Corridor: 5.1 m long, 1.8 m wide, 1.8 m high * Entrance: 1.9 m wide, 2.4 m high The chamber features massive basalt stones. A rectangular slab (2.55 m long, 0.75 m wide, 0.13 m thick) within the main chamber suggests the presence of a stone sarcophagus. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Iki City Ikikoku Museum|ja|壱岐市立一支国博物館}} (Iki City, Ashibe Town) == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Ashibe Town Board of Education, ''Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun Environmental Improvement Project Report,'' 1990. * Iki City Board of Education, ''Kofun Groups on Iki Island: Current Survey,'' 2012. * Kyushu University, ''Archaeological Report of Ikihara Sites,'' 2018. * Togo Yoshida, ''The Great Japanese Gazetteer, Volume 7: Ōu,'' 1902. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Oni-no-iwaya Kofun (Iki)}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/645 Iki Kofun Group - Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage Database] [[Category:Iki Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Iki City)]][[Category:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Iki City)]][[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Iki City)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[ja:%E9%AC%BC%E3%81%AE%E7%AA%9F%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3_(%E5%A3%B1%E5%B2%90%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:鬼の窟古墳]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Iki City)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Oni-no-Iwaya City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Iki City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Iki City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] {{Kofun navbar}}
